seo.title: Fintech Voice Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for voice agents AI agents in fintech. Covers SOX, PCI DSS, GDPR.

content.intro: Deploying voice AI agents in fintech environments introduces compounding risks across real-time audio pipelines, financial data handling, and regulatory accountability — where a single misrouted instruction or unencrypted transcript can trigger SEC enforcement or PCI DSS violations. This checklist addresses the full lifecycle of a voice agent deployment, from speech-to-text ingestion through LLM reasoning to audio output, with controls mapped directly to SOX, PCI DSS, GDPR, BSA/AML, and SEC requirements. Given that failure consequences include loss of banking licenses and customer fund mismanagement, every item here is treated as a production-critical control, not a best-practice suggestion.

sections.heading: Data Security & Encryption Controls
sections.description: Voice agents in fintech process spoken PAN data, account numbers, and transaction instructions in real time. Every stage of the STT → LLM → TTS pipeline must enforce encryption and data minimization to satisfy PCI DSS Level 1 and GDPR Article 25 requirements.

items.label: Encrypt audio streams in transit with TLS 1.3
items.description: All raw audio payloads transmitted between the microphone client, STT service, and LLM backend must use TLS 1.3 minimum. Validate cipher suites and certificate pinning on mobile and web clients to prevent MITM interception of spoken card numbers or account credentials.

items.label: Encrypt STT transcripts at rest using AES-256
items.description: Transcribed text containing financial instructions or PII must be stored with AES-256 encryption in your data store. Apply envelope encryption with customer-managed keys (CMK) in your KMS to satisfy PCI DSS Requirement 3.5 and enable GDPR right-to-erasure by key deletion.

items.label: Implement PAN masking in STT output before LLM ingestion
items.description: Deploy a real-time regex and ML-based PAN/CVV detection layer between the STT output and the LLM prompt. Replace detected card numbers with tokenized placeholders (e.g., CARD_TOKEN_XXXX) before the transcript enters the LLM context window to prevent model memorization of cardholder data.

items.label: Define and enforce audio transcript retention limits
items.description: Set a maximum retention period for raw audio recordings and STT transcripts — typically 90 days for dispute resolution, then automated deletion. Document this policy in your GDPR Record of Processing Activities (RoPA) and configure automated purge jobs with audit logs confirming deletion.

items.label: Isolate voice agent infrastructure in a PCI DSS-scoped network segment
items.description: Place all STT, LLM inference, and TTS components within a dedicated network segment subject to PCI DSS scoping. Use firewall rules to restrict inbound/outbound traffic to only required endpoints, and conduct quarterly network segmentation tests as required by PCI DSS Requirement 11.4.

items.label: Tokenize account identifiers before passing to LLM context
items.description: Replace full account numbers, SSNs, and routing numbers with reversible tokens from a secure vault (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) before constructing LLM prompts. Maintain a token-to-value mapping accessible only to authorized backend services, never exposed in voice agent logs.

items.label: Audit all data access to voice transcripts under SOX controls
items.description: Implement immutable audit logs for every access to stored voice transcripts and derived financial data, capturing user ID, timestamp, action, and data classification. These logs must be tamper-evident and retained for 7 years to satisfy SOX Section 802 record retention requirements.

sections.heading: Regulatory Compliance & Financial Advice Guardrails
sections.description: Voice agents that discuss account balances, investment options, or credit products are subject to SEC regulations, FINRA suitability rules, and BSA/AML monitoring obligations. The LLM reasoning layer must be constrained to prevent unlicensed financial advice and ensure every applicable disclaimer is delivered verbally.

items.label: Inject mandatory verbal disclaimers for investment-related responses
items.description: Configure a post-processing filter that detects investment, securities, or retirement-related LLM outputs and prepends a required verbal disclaimer (e.g., 'This is not personalized investment advice. Consult a licensed financial advisor.'). Log disclaimer delivery with a timestamp for SEC examination readiness.

items.label: Block LLM from providing specific securities recommendations
items.description: Implement a topic classifier that intercepts LLM outputs containing specific stock tickers, fund recommendations, or buy/sell signals before TTS synthesis. Route these queries to a licensed advisor handoff flow rather than generating a direct voice response, preventing SEC Rule 206(4)-7 violations.

items.label: Implement real-time BSA/AML transaction monitoring on voice-initiated transfers
items.description: Route all fund transfer instructions captured via voice through your existing AML screening engine (e.g., NICE Actimize, Oracle FCCM) before execution. Flag transactions matching structuring patterns or OFAC watchlist entities and suspend the voice session pending compliance review.

items.label: Enforce credit decision explainability for voice-initiated loan queries
items.description: When the voice agent communicates a credit decision or pre-qualification result, the LLM must generate a plain-language explanation of the primary factors (e.g., 'Your application was declined primarily due to a high debt-to-income ratio'). Store the explanation alongside the decision record to satisfy ECOA adverse action notice requirements.

items.label: Record and archive all voice sessions involving financial transactions
items.description: Capture full audio recordings of voice sessions where transactions are initiated, modified, or cancelled. Archive recordings in a WORM-compliant storage system for a minimum of 3 years per SEC Rule 17a-4, with metadata tagging for customer ID, session ID, and transaction reference.

items.label: Validate agent identity disclosure at session start
items.description: Ensure the voice agent identifies itself as an AI system within the first 10 seconds of every session, including its name and the institution it represents. This satisfies FTC disclosure requirements and CFPB guidance on automated customer interactions, and must be logged as a compliance event.

items.label: Implement Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) triggers for anomalous voice patterns
items.description: Deploy behavioral analytics on voice session metadata (e.g., unusual transaction velocity, off-hours access, atypical geographic patterns) to auto-generate SAR filing candidates. Integrate with your BSA compliance workflow so flagged sessions are reviewed within the 30-day SAR filing window.

sections.heading: Real-Time Pipeline Performance & Safety Filtering
sections.description: Sub-second latency requirements in the STT → LLM → TTS pipeline create pressure to skip safety checks, but in fintech this is unacceptable. Safety filters, latency budgets, and fallback mechanisms must be engineered as first-class pipeline components, not afterthoughts.

items.label: Define and enforce per-stage latency budgets with circuit breakers
items.description: Allocate explicit latency budgets: STT ≤150ms, LLM inference ≤400ms, safety filter ≤50ms, TTS synthesis ≤200ms. Implement circuit breakers that trigger a pre-recorded fallback response ('I'm having trouble processing that, please try again or press 0 for an agent') if any stage exceeds its budget, preventing silent failures.

items.label: Deploy a dedicated financial safety classifier on LLM outputs
items.description: Run a lightweight, fine-tuned classifier (≤30ms inference) on every LLM response before TTS synthesis to detect hallucinated account balances, fabricated interest rates, or unauthorized transaction confirmations. Block and log any response scoring above the risk threshold, routing the session to a human agent.

items.label: Implement conversation coherence validation across multi-turn sessions
items.description: Maintain a session state tracker that validates each LLM response against the established conversation context — detecting contradictions (e.g., confirming a transfer amount that differs from what was spoken) or topic drift into unauthorized domains. Flag incoherent turns for immediate session review.

items.label: Test STT accuracy on financial terminology and accented speech
items.description: Conduct quarterly accuracy benchmarks of your STT model on a curated test set of financial terms (routing numbers, IBAN formats, fund names, regulatory acronyms) across at least 5 accent profiles. Require ≥98% word error rate accuracy on financial entities before any model update is promoted to production.

items.label: Implement voice authentication with liveness detection before high-risk actions
items.description: Require voice biometric authentication with anti-spoofing liveness detection (replay attack and deepfake detection) before executing transactions above a defined threshold (e.g., >$500 transfers). Integrate with your existing MFA framework and log authentication outcomes for SOX access control audits.

items.label: Configure automatic session termination on repeated safety filter triggers
items.description: Define a policy that terminates a voice session and escalates to a human agent after 2 consecutive safety filter blocks within a single session. Log the full session transcript, filter trigger reasons, and escalation outcome for post-incident review and model improvement.

items.label: Validate TTS output does not introduce financial misinformation
items.description: Implement a round-trip validation step that runs STT on the TTS audio output and compares the re-transcribed text against the original LLM response for material discrepancies (e.g., number misreading, currency symbol errors). Reject and regenerate audio where discrepancies exceed a 2% character error rate on financial figures.

items.label: Load test the full voice pipeline at 10x peak transaction volume
items.description: Before production launch and after any major model update, conduct load tests simulating 10x your expected peak concurrent voice sessions. Measure p95 and p99 latency, safety filter bypass rates under load, and error rates. Require all metrics to remain within SLA bounds before approving deployment.

sections.heading: Governance, Auditability & Incident Response
sections.description: Fintech voice agents must be governed with the same rigor as any other financial system of record. Model decisions, session logs, and safety interventions must be auditable by regulators, and incident response plans must account for the unique failure modes of real-time AI pipelines.

items.label: Maintain a model card and version registry for all LLM and STT components
items.description: Document every model version deployed in the voice pipeline in a centralized registry, including training data provenance, evaluation benchmarks, known limitations, and approval sign-offs. This registry must be producible within 24 hours for SEC or OCC examination requests and updated with every model change.

items.label: Implement immutable decision logs for all voice-initiated financial actions
items.description: Write an immutable log entry for every financial action initiated via voice (transfers, payments, account changes), capturing session ID, anonymized transcript excerpt, LLM reasoning summary, safety filter outcome, and execution result. Store in a WORM-compliant system and index for regulatory query response.

items.label: Define a voice agent-specific incident response runbook
items.description: Create a runbook covering: (1) immediate session termination procedures for compromised voice agents, (2) rollback steps for LLM model versions, (3) customer notification templates for voice-initiated fraud, (4) regulatory notification timelines (72-hour GDPR breach notification, SAR filing deadlines). Test the runbook in a tabletop exercise quarterly.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red team exercises targeting voice agent manipulation
items.description: Engage a specialized red team to attempt prompt injection via spoken input, voice spoofing attacks, social engineering the LLM into bypassing financial controls, and latency-based safety filter evasion. Document findings, remediation timelines, and retest results in your risk register.

items.label: Establish a human escalation SLA for voice agent failures
items.description: Define and contractually commit to a maximum wait time for human agent escalation when the voice AI fails or is terminated (e.g., ≤90 seconds). Monitor escalation queue depth in real time and alert operations teams when SLA breach risk is detected, ensuring customers are never stranded mid-transaction.

items.label: Perform annual third-party AI model risk assessment per SR 11-7 guidance
items.description: Commission an independent model risk assessment of all LLM and STT components against the Federal Reserve's SR 11-7 Model Risk Management guidance. The assessment must cover conceptual soundness, ongoing monitoring, and outcomes analysis. Findings must be reported to your Model Risk Committee and tracked to remediation.

items.label: Implement GDPR data subject rights workflows for voice data
items.description: Build automated workflows to handle GDPR Article 15 (access), Article 17 (erasure), and Article 20 (portability) requests for voice recordings and transcripts. Ensure erasure requests are fulfilled within 30 days, including deletion from STT training data pipelines, and generate a deletion certificate for audit purposes.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: PCI DSS Requirement 4.2.1 mandates that all PAN data transmitted over open networks — including voice-to-text audio streams containing spoken card numbers — must be encrypted with strong cryptography. Fintech voice agents must implement real-time PAN detection and tokenization in the STT output layer before any data reaches the LLM, and must include voice agent infrastructure within the annual PCI DSS scoping assessment and QSA audit.

SEC Rule 17a-4 and FINRA Rule 4511 require broker-dealers to retain records of all communications related to securities transactions, including AI-generated voice interactions, in a WORM-compliant format for a minimum of 3 years (6 years for certain records). Voice session recordings, LLM decision logs, and safety filter outcomes for investment-related queries must be archived accordingly and producible within 24 hours of an SEC examination request.

GDPR Article 22 grants data subjects the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with significant legal or financial effects. Voice agents that make or communicate credit decisions, account restrictions, or fraud flags must either involve meaningful human review or provide a clear mechanism for the customer to request human intervention — and this right must be disclosed verbally at the point of automated decision delivery.

BSA/AML regulations (31 CFR Chapter X) require financial institutions to apply Customer Due Diligence and transaction monitoring to all channels, including AI voice agents. Voice-initiated transactions must be screened against OFAC SDN lists in real time before execution, and the institution must demonstrate to FinCEN examiners that the voice channel is fully integrated into the enterprise-wide AML program, with SAR filing procedures that account for AI-detected suspicious patterns.

cta.heading: Automate Your Fintech Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your fintech voice agent pipeline against these controls — automatically flagging PCI DSS violations, safety filter gaps, and regulatory drift before your next examination.
